display_name: Prefix-list matches on prefix length (le/ge), not on address bits like an ACL — confusing the two causes silent filtering failures
context: Configuring route filtering on BGP or OSPF using a prefix-list, and routes are unexpectedly permitted or denied
observation: A prefix-list entry like 'permit 10.0.0.0/8 le 24' is configured for route filtering, but specific /16 or /24 subnets within 10.0.0.0/8 are being filtered differently than expected — some are matched and some are not, or matching seems inconsistent compared to how an ACL would work
naive_interpretation: The prefix-list works like an ACL with a wildcard mask — 'permit 10.0.0.0/8' matches any address that starts with 10, and 'le 24' means something about the host portion
correct_interpretation: A prefix-list matches on TWO dimensions simultaneously: the network address AND the prefix length (subnet mask). 'permit 10.0.0.0/8 le 24' means: match any route whose network address falls within 10.0.0.0/8 AND whose prefix length is between /8 and /24 inclusive. A route for 10.1.0.0/26 would NOT match because /26 is longer than /24, even though 10.1.0.0 is within 10.0.0.0/8. Without 'le' or 'ge', a prefix-list entry is an EXACT match on both the network and the mask: 'permit 10.0.0.0/8' matches ONLY the exact prefix 10.0.0.0/8, NOT 10.1.0.0/24 or 10.0.0.0/16. This is fundamentally different from an ACL, where '10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255' matches any PACKET with a source or destination in that range, regardless of subnet mask — ACLs have no concept of prefix length because they match packet headers, not routing table entries.
why: ACLs match packet headers (source/destination IP address) using address bits and wildcard masks — they operate at the data plane on individual packets and have no knowledge of the routing table
Prefix-lists match routing table entries (network + prefix length pairs) — they operate at the control plane on route advertisements, where the subnet mask is a key part of the route identity
The 'le' (less-or-equal) and 'ge' (greater-or-equal) modifiers define a range of acceptable prefix lengths — without them, only the exact prefix length matches

The most common mistake is writing 'permit 10.0.0.0/8' expecting it to match all subnets of 10.0.0.0/8 — it only matches the single exact route 10.0.0.0/8; use 'permit 10.0.0.0/8 le 32' to match all subnets

confirmation_commands.purpose: Display the prefix-list entries with their le/ge modifiers to verify matching criteria
Test whether a specific prefix matches any entry in the prefix-list
Display prefix-list entries with hit counts to see which entries are matching traffic
Verify the exact prefix and mask in the BGP table to confirm what the prefix-list is matching against
Display prefix-list entries and verify the le/ge range

traps: Writing 'permit 10.0.0.0/8' and expecting it to match all subnets within 10.0.0.0/8 — without le/ge it is an EXACT match for the /8 route only
Confusing prefix-list le/ge with ACL wildcard masks — 'le 24' is about prefix LENGTH, not about which address bits to ignore
Using 'ge 24 le 32' to match host routes and specific subnets but forgetting that 'ge' sets the MINIMUM prefix length — 'ge 24' excludes /8 through /23
Not realizing that 'permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 32' is the prefix-list equivalent of 'permit any' — it matches every prefix of every length

Applying an ACL instead of a prefix-list in a route-map 'match ip address' clause when the intent is to filter by prefix length — ACLs in route-maps match the prefix as if it were a packet, ignoring the mask entirely on some platforms
